title: Children who experience violent discipline vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Percentage of children (2–14) who according to self-reports by caregivers, experienced any violent discipline at home in the past month. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: GDP per capita is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices.
caption:
Children who experience violent discipline vs. GDP per capita

Percentage of children (2–14) who according to self-reports by caregivers, experienced any violent discipline at home in the past month. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the percentage of children aged 2 to 14 who experienced violent discipline in the past month and the GDP per capita for various countries in 2015. The vertical axis measures the percentage of children experiencing violent discipline, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, spanning from $0 to over $30,000. Each circle represents a country, with its size proportional to the total population. The data points are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A general trend is visible where countries with lower GDP per capita, particularly those below $10,000, tend to cluster in the upper portion of the chart, indicating higher percentages of violent discipline. For instance, Ghana, Nigeria, Bangladesh, and Egypt are positioned in the top-left quadrant, showing percentages between 80% and nearly 100% alongside lower GDP figures. Conversely, countries with higher GDP per capita, such as Argentina, Belarus, and Kazakhstan, are located toward the right side of the chart and generally exhibit lower percentages, mostly falling between 40% and 75%. Several nations in Europe and Asia, including Ukraine, Serbia, and Mongolia, occupy the middle range of the GDP axis with percentages varying between 40% and 70%. The chart visualizes a negative correlation where higher economic output per person often aligns with lower reported rates of violent discipline against children.
